title: The XOR Problem
caption: Marvin Minsky, co-author of Perceptrons (1969), photographed at the KI 2006 conference in Bremen.

overview.text: In their 1969 book Perceptrons, Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert delivered a rigorous mathematical critique of single-layer neural networks. Their central result was damning: a single-layer perceptron cannot learn the XOR function, because XOR is not linearly separable. The book's influence — combined with dwindling results from early AI programs — helped trigger the first 'AI winter,' sharply cutting funding and enthusiasm for neural network research throughout the 1970s.

deep.text: Minsky and Papert proved that any function a single-layer perceptron can compute must be linearly separable — expressible by a single hyperplane boundary in input space. XOR maps (0,0)→0, (1,1)→0, (0,1)→1, (1,0)→1; no straight line separates the two classes. The solution — adding hidden layers — was known in principle but lacked a practical training algorithm until backpropagation arrived in 1986.
deep.math: XOR(x₁, x₂) = 1  iff  x₁ ≠ x₂  (no linear boundary separates these four points)

challenge.statement: Minsky and Papert's 1969 book proved that no neural network, of any size or depth, can learn the XOR function.

feedback.correct: Correct — it's a myth. They proved single-layer perceptrons cannot solve XOR. Multi-layer networks can, but lacked a training algorithm at the time.
feedback.incorrect: Actually this is a myth. Minsky and Papert showed single-layer perceptrons fail at XOR, not all networks. A two-layer network solves it easily.

sources.title: Perceptrons: An Introduction to Computational Geometry
sources.author: M. Minsky & S. Papert
